schools.summary: Gill: the words of the Song.
Keil & Delitzsch: the Song embraces the whole future history of Israel, a prophetic testimony from the mouth of Moses.

themes: Give ear, O ye heavens, and I will speak; and hear, O earth
heaven and earth summoned to witness
hear, O heavens; for the LORD hath spoken (Isa. 1:2)

schools.summary: Gill: some take it as a wish that the doctrine might fall like rain and dew on men, penetrate their hearts, and produce good effects.

themes: My doctrine shall drop as the rain, my speech shall distil as the dew
God's word a gracious, fruitful refreshment
so shall my word be that goeth forth (Isa. 55:10-11)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses proposed to proclaim the name of the LORD, the same proclaimed before him (Exod. 34:6), understood of Jehovah the Son, the Rock; greatness to be ascribed to our God.

themes: Because I will publish the name of the LORD: ascribe ye greatness unto our God
the chief end the glory of his name
give the LORD the glory due unto his name (Ps. 29:2)

schools.summary: Gill: Jehovah is the Rock, our God, even Christ the rock of salvation; the first word so emphatic that one letter is larger than usual; his work perfect, his ways judgment, a God of truth and without iniquity.

themes: He is the Rock, his work is perfect... a God of truth and without iniquity, just and right is he
the perfection and faithfulness of God
that Rock was Christ (1 Cor. 10:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the characters of the enemies of Christ, set in contrast with him — corrupt not by nature only but in their tenets and principles, a perverse and crooked generation.

themes: They have corrupted themselves... they are a perverse and crooked generation
corruption self-wrought, set against the Rock
a crooked and perverse nation (Phil. 2:15)

schools.summary: Gill: a proper character of the Jews in the times of Christ, often called 'fools,' ignorant of the Scriptures, setting their traditions above the word; he is their father that bought and made them.

themes: is not he thy father that hath bought thee? hath he not made thee
the baseness of requiting redeeming love
if I be a father, where is mine honour? (Mal. 1:6)

schools.summary: Gill: the remembering of the days of old and the instances of divine goodness, the fathers and elders telling them.

themes: Remember the days of old... ask thy father, and he will shew thee
the memory of God's mercies a teacher
our fathers have told us thy work (Ps. 44:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the dividing of the earth in the days of Peleg, the Most High giving every nation its part, setting the bounds according to the children of Israel.

themes: When the Most High divided to the nations their inheritance... he set the bounds... according to the children of Israel
God ordering the nations with his people in view
the bounds of their habitation set by him (Acts 17:26)

schools.summary: Gill: why the Lord so early provided a portion for Israel — because they were his part, his inheritance, which he chose by lot for himself.

themes: For the LORD's portion is his people; Jacob is the lot of his inheritance
God's electing love the ground of his care
the LORD hath chosen Jacob for his peculiar treasure (Ps. 135:4)

schools.summary: Gill: instances of God's goodness in the wilderness, where Israel was in a most forlorn condition, and the Lord found and kept them as the apple of his eye.

themes: he led him about, he instructed him, he kept him as the apple of his eye
the surpassing tenderness of God's care
he that toucheth you toucheth the apple of his eye (Zech. 2:8)

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: the eagle is merciful to its young, rousing them with a noise and her wings, that they be fitter to receive her, and bearing them on her wings.

themes: As an eagle stirreth up her nest, fluttereth over her young... beareth them on her wings
God's care uniting tenderness and discipline
I bare you on eagles' wings (Exod. 19:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord alone led them out of Egypt, through the wilderness, to Canaan, going before them in the pillar of cloud and fire; no strange god with him.

themes: So the LORD alone did lead him, and there was no strange god with him
the whole glory of their leading God's alone
beside me there is no saviour (Isa. 43:11)

schools.summary: Gill: the high places of Canaan, the strongholds delivered into their hands, and the increase of the fields; honey out of the rock and oil out of the flinty rock.

themes: He made him ride on the high places of the earth... and he made him to suck honey out of the rock
the lavishness of God's provision
honey out of the rock should I have satisfied thee (Ps. 81:16)

schools.summary: Gill: the butter of kine, the cream gathered on the milk, the butter of the ancients.
Keil & Delitzsch: the milk of sheep, fat of lambs, and rams of the breed of Bashan.

themes: Butter of kine, and milk of sheep, with fat of lambs... and thou didst drink the pure blood of the grape
God's abundant goodness to his people
thy paths drop fatness (Ps. 65:11)

schools.summary: Gill: Jeshurun is a name of Israel, from a word meaning upright, such as they ought to have been; he forsook God and lightly esteemed the Rock of his salvation.

themes: Jeshurun waxed fat, and kicked... then he forsook God which made him, and lightly esteemed the Rock of his salvation
the deadly fruit of prosperity abused
lest when full thou forget the LORD (Deut. 8:12-14)

schools.summary: Gill: the word may be read 'with others'; the Jews after the Babylonish captivity were not guilty of worshipping idols; Cocceius takes it of another worship.

themes: They provoked him to jealousy with strange gods, with abominations provoked they him to anger
idolatry the spiritual adultery rousing God's jealousy
moved him to jealousy with their graven images (Ps. 78:58)

schools.summary: Gill: their sacrifices, continued when God willed them to cease, were reckoned not as offered to him but to demons, under the instigation of Satan.

themes: They sacrificed unto devils, not to God; to gods whom they knew not
idolatry the service of demons
the Gentiles sacrifice to devils (1 Cor. 10:20)

schools.summary: Gill: the same as the rock of salvation, repeated that their ingratitude be observed; begetting ascribed to this Rock, as regeneration is to Christ; they forgot the God that formed them.

themes: Of the Rock that begat thee thou art unmindful, and hast forgotten God that formed thee
the heinous ingratitude of forgetting their Maker
they forgat God their saviour (Ps. 106:21)

schools.summary: Gill: the disregard and rejection of Christ, their forgetfulness and contempt of him; he abhorred them for the provoking of his sons and daughters.

themes: when the LORD saw it, he abhorred them, because of the provoking of his sons, and of his daughters
God most provoked by the sins of his own
I nourished children, and they rebelled (Isa. 1:2)

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord proceeds to pass sentence for their ill treatment of his Son; they were a froward generation, children in whom is no faith.

themes: I will hide my face from them... for they are a very froward generation, children in whom is no faith
the withdrawal of God's favour the heaviest stroke
thou didst hide thy face, and I was troubled (Ps. 30:7)

schools.summary: Gill: provoking with a false messiah, and God moving them to jealousy with the calling of the Gentiles, who were not a people, a foolish nation.

themes: they have moved me to jealousy with that which is not God... I will move them to jealousy with those which are not a people
a fitting retribution in kind
the Gentiles called to provoke them to jealousy (Rom. 10:19)

schools.summary: Gill: here begins the account of temporal judgments on the Jews for rejecting the Messiah, the destruction of Judea and burning of Jerusalem.
Keil & Delitzsch: the fire burns to the lowest hell and consumes the earth with her increase.

themes: a fire is kindled in mine anger, and shall burn unto the lowest hell
the terrible intensity of God's wrath
our God is a consuming fire (Heb. 12:29)

schools.summary: Gill: one calamity upon another, particularly mentioned in the verses following.
Keil & Delitzsch: the heaping of evils and the spending of his arrows upon them.

themes: I will heap mischiefs upon them; I will spend mine arrows upon them
an accumulation of judgments, each finding its mark
the arrows of the Almighty (Job 6:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the arrow of famine, whose force makes the skin black as if burnt; Onkelos reads 'swelled with famine,' as Josephus describes the siege of Jerusalem; the teeth of beasts and poison of serpents.

themes: They shall be burnt with hunger... I will also send the teeth of beasts upon them, with the poison of serpents
the manifold means of God's judgment
wild beasts and the sword upon the rebellious (Lev. 26:22-25)

schools.summary: Gill: the sword without, of the Roman army besieging, or of the seditious within the streets, destroying multitudes among themselves.
Keil & Delitzsch: the sword without and terror within.

themes: The sword without, and terror within, shall destroy both the young man and the virgin
a destruction complete and inescapable
the sword without, at home as death (Lam. 1:20)

schools.summary: Gill: God could have said, or might have determined, what was in his power and in right and justice to do.
Keil & Delitzsch: the threat to scatter them and make their remembrance cease.

themes: I said, I would scatter them into corners, I would make the remembrance of them to cease
the extremity of deserved judgment, withheld by mercy
except the LORD had left a remnant (Isa. 1:9)

schools.summary: That God restrained his judgment lest the enemy boast: 'were it not that I feared the wrath of the enemy, lest their adversaries should behave themselves strangely, and lest they should say, Our hand is high, and the LORD hath not done all this' — God would have utterly cut Israel off but for the danger that the enemy would boast it was their own doing (the were it not that I feared the wrath of the enemy, God's regard for the enemy's pride, the lest their adversaries should behave themselves strangely, the enemy misreading the judgment, the lest they should say, Our hand is high, and the LORD hath not done all this, the enemy's boast, the figure, God restraining his judgment on Israel lest the heathen ascribe it to their own power, the truth that God spares his people partly for his own glory, lest the enemy blaspheme, the figure of the divine concern for his own honour among the nations, the enemy's boast forestalled, 'I wrought for my name's sake, that it should not be polluted before the heathen,' Ezekiel 20:9, the restraint that guards God's glory, the judgment tempered for his name's sake); God restraining judgment lest the enemy misjudge (the honour of God, Ezekiel 20:9) — the restraint (lest the enemy boast); 'lest they should say, Our hand is high, and the LORD hath not done all this' (the enemy's boast that their own hand had done it, the blasphemy forestalled, the figure, God's concern for his own honour, the providence, the restraint, the people's lack of understanding to follow, vv.28-29) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Were it not that I feared the wrath of the enemy, lest their adversaries should behave themselves strangely, and lest they should say, Our hand is high, and the LORD hath not done all this
that God restrained his judgment lest the enemy boast
God restraining his judgment on Israel lest the heathen ascribe it to their own power
sparing his people for his own name's sake (Ezekiel 20:9)

schools.summary: That the nation was void of counsel and understanding: 'for they are a nation void of counsel, neither is there any understanding in them' — Israel (or the enemy) is described as without wisdom or understanding (the for they are a nation void of counsel, without wisdom, the neither is there any understanding in them, without understanding, the figure, the folly of the people who cannot discern God's hand, void of counsel, the truth that the lack of understanding is the root of their ruin, the people unwise, the figure of the spiritual blindness that cannot perceive God's ways, 'O that they were wise, that they understood this,' Deuteronomy 32:29, the want of wisdom that fails to see the cause of their calamity, the folly that does not consider the latter end, vv.29); the people without wisdom (the folly) — the folly (void of counsel); 'neither is there any understanding in them' (the people without understanding, the spiritual blindness, the figure, the want of wisdom to perceive God's hand, the providence, the folly, the wish that they would consider their latter end to follow, vv.29) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For they are a nation void of counsel, neither is there any understanding in them
that the nation was void of counsel and understanding
the people without wisdom or understanding, unable to discern God's hand in their calamity
the folly that is the root of their ruin

schools.summary: The wish that they were wise to consider their latter end: 'O that they were wise, that they understood this, that they would consider their latter end!' — a wish that the people had wisdom to understand and consider what their course would bring (the O that they were wise, that they understood this, the wish for wisdom, the that they would consider their latter end!, that they weigh the end, the figure, the longing that the people would be wise and consider where their sin would lead, the truth that true wisdom considers the latter end, the consequences of one's ways, the figure of the wisdom that weighs the end, 'so teach us to number our days, that we may apply our hearts unto wisdom,' Psalm 90:12, the want of consideration that ruins, the wise weighing the end, the call to consider the outcome of sin, vv.28); the wish that they had wisdom to understand (the longing, Psalm 90:12) — the wish (for wisdom); 'that they would consider their latter end!' (the weighing of the end the mark of wisdom, the consideration, the figure, the wisdom that weighs the consequences, the providence, the wish, the enemy's superiority but for God to follow, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: O that they were wise, that they understood this, that they would consider their latter end!
the wish that they were wise to consider their latter end
the longing that the people had wisdom to understand and weigh where their sin would lead
true wisdom considering the latter end (Psalm 90:12)

schools.summary: That their defeat could only be because God had given them up: 'how should one chase a thousand, and two put ten thousand to flight, except their Rock had sold them, and the LORD had shut them up?' — such a rout of Israel could only happen because God had abandoned them (the how should one chase a thousand, and two put ten thousand to flight, the impossible rout, the except their Rock had sold them, and the LORD had shut them up?, unless God had given them up, the figure, the defeat of the many by the few only possible because God had forsaken his people, the truth that Israel's strength was God, and only his withdrawal could explain their rout, the figure of the people's helplessness without God, the Rock who sold them, the reversal of the promise that one should chase a thousand, now turned against them, 'five of you shall chase an hundred,' Leviticus 26:8, the defeat that proved God had given them up, the Rock forsaken, the people shut up); the impossible rout of the many by the few (the defeat, Leviticus 26:8) — the rout (one chasing a thousand); 'except their Rock had sold them, and the LORD had shut them up?' (the defeat only because God gave them up, the Rock forsaken, the figure, the people's helplessness without God, the providence, the defeat, the contrast of the rocks to follow, vv.31) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: How should one chase a thousand, and two put ten thousand to flight, except their Rock had sold them, and the LORD had shut them up?
that their defeat could only be because God had given them up
such a rout of Israel possible only because God had abandoned them
the Rock who was their strength having given them up (Leviticus 26:8)

schools.summary: The confession that the false rocks of the nations are nothing compared to the Rock of Israel: 'for their rock is not as our Rock, even our enemies themselves being judges' — the incomparable Rock — 'for their rock is not as our Rock, even our enemies themselves being judges': the gods (the 'rock') in which the heathen trusted are no match for the LORD, the true Rock of Israel, as even the enemies must own (the witnesses noting the significance — the surpassing greatness of the true God above all false gods, the Rock of Israel beyond compare, the idols the nations called their rock are vanity beside the living God, even the enemies being judges of the difference, the lesson — that God alone is the true Rock, the strength and refuge of his people, immeasurably above the false rocks of the nations, the contrast between the dead idols and the living God, the one a delusion, the other the everlasting strength, the truth that the LORD is the Rock, sure and immovable, 'he only is my rock and my salvation,' Psalm 62:2, 'the LORD is my rock, and my fortress,' Psalm 18:2, the figure — Christ the Rock, the sure foundation of his people, 'that Rock was Christ,' 1 Corinthians 10:4, the truth that the believer's confidence rests on the unfailing Rock, not the broken reeds of the world, the figure of the incomparable God, the Rock above all rocks, the false gods owning their inferiority, the warning against trusting in any rock but the true, the supremacy of the God of Israel that even the heathen cannot deny, the figure of the incomparable God as the true Rock of his people, above all false rocks); the false rocks of the nations as nothing beside the LORD, the true Rock of Israel (the incomparable God, Psalm 62:2) — the contrast (their rock vs our Rock); 'even our enemies themselves being judges' (even the enemies must own the difference, the supremacy of the true Rock undeniable, Christ the Rock of his people, the sure foundation, Psalm 18:2; 1 Corinthians 10:4) — the figure (Christ the Rock, the sure foundation, the providence, the contrast, the corruption of the false rocks to follow, vv.32-33) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'their rock is not as our Rock' establish?

sides.position: The incomparable God as the true Rock of his people - the false gods of the nations vanity beside the living God, the LORD the sure and immovable Rock and refuge (Psalm 62:2; Psalm 18:2), a figure of Christ the Rock, the sure foundation (1 Corinthians 10:4), the supremacy of the God of Israel that even the enemies must own

themes: For their rock is not as our Rock, even our enemies themselves being judges
the confession that the false rocks of the nations are nothing compared to the Rock of Israel (the crux of the incomparable God as the true Rock of his people)
the surpassing greatness of the true God above all false gods, the idols the nations call their rock vanity beside the living God, the LORD the sure and immovable Rock and refuge
a figure of Christ the Rock, the sure foundation, the supremacy even the enemies must own (Psalm 62:2; Psalm 18:2; 1 Corinthians 10:4)

schools.summary: The corruption of the false rocks, the vine of Sodom: 'for their vine is of the vine of Sodom, and of the fields of Gomorrah: their grapes are grapes of gall, their clusters are bitter' — the nations (or the corrupt people) are likened to the vine of Sodom, producing bitter and poisonous fruit (the for their vine is of the vine of Sodom, and of the fields of Gomorrah, the vine of Sodom, the their grapes are grapes of gall, their clusters are bitter, the bitter fruit, the figure, the corruption of the wicked likened to the cursed vine of Sodom, bringing forth poison, the truth that the fruit of the ungodly is bitter and deadly, as the vine of the doomed cities, the figure of the evil that produces nothing but gall and bitterness, 'do men gather grapes of thorns?' Matthew 7:16, the wickedness that yields poisonous fruit, the vine of Sodom, the corruption that ends in bitterness, vv.33); the corruption likened to the vine of Sodom (the wickedness, Matthew 7:16) — the vine (of Sodom); 'their grapes are grapes of gall, their clusters are bitter' (the bitter and poisonous fruit, the deadly produce, the figure, the evil that yields gall, the providence, the corruption, the poison of dragons to follow, vv.33) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For their vine is of the vine of Sodom, and of the fields of Gomorrah: their grapes are grapes of gall, their clusters are bitter
the corruption of the false rocks, the vine of Sodom
the corruption of the ungodly likened to the cursed vine of Sodom
bringing forth nothing but gall and bitterness (Matthew 7:16)

schools.summary: Their wine as the poison of dragons: 'their wine is the poison of dragons, and the cruel venom of asps' — the fruit of their corruption is likened to deadly poison (the their wine is the poison of dragons, the poison of dragons, the and the cruel venom of asps, the venom of asps, the figure, the corruption of the wicked likened to deadly venom, their wine poison, the truth that the produce of the ungodly is not refreshment but poison, deadly to the soul, the figure of the deadly fruit of sin, the wine of wickedness venom, 'the poison of asps is under their lips,' Romans 3:13, the corruption that poisons, the venom of the wicked, the deadly end of the false vine, vv.32); their wine likened to deadly poison (the venom, Romans 3:13) — the poison (of dragons); 'and the cruel venom of asps' (the cruel venom of the wicked, the deadly produce, the figure, the deadly fruit of sin, the providence, the poison, God's judgment laid up to follow, vv.34-35) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Their wine is the poison of dragons, and the cruel venom of asps
their wine as the poison of dragons
the fruit of the wicked's corruption likened to deadly venom, not refreshment but poison
the deadly end of the false vine (Romans 3:13)

schools.summary: That God's judgment is laid up and sealed with him: 'is not this laid up in store with me, and sealed up among my treasures?' — the sin of the wicked, and the vengeance for it, are stored and sealed with God for the appointed day (the is not this laid up in store with me, laid up with God, the and sealed up among my treasures?, sealed for the day, the figure, the sin and the vengeance laid up and sealed, kept till the day of recompence, the truth that God remembers and stores up the wickedness of the wicked, the judgment reserved, the figure of the treasured-up wrath against the day of judgment, 'treasurest up unto thyself wrath against the day of wrath,' Romans 2:5, the vengeance kept in store, sealed and sure, the judgment laid up with God, vv.35); the sin and vengeance laid up with God (the reserved judgment, Romans 2:5) — the store (laid up); 'and sealed up among my treasures?' (the vengeance sealed for the appointed day, the judgment reserved, the figure, the treasured-up wrath against the day of judgment, the providence, the store, the day of vengeance to follow, vv.35) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Is not this laid up in store with me, and sealed up among my treasures?
that God's judgment is laid up and sealed with him
the sin of the wicked and the vengeance for it stored and sealed with God for the appointed day
the judgment reserved and sure (Romans 2:5)

schools.summary: God's declaration that vengeance and recompence belong to him: 'to me belongeth vengeance, and recompence; their foot shall slide in due time: for the day of their calamity is at hand, and the things that shall come upon them make haste' — the divine prerogative — 'to me belongeth vengeance, and recompence; their foot shall slide in due time: for the day of their calamity is at hand': vengeance and recompence are God's alone, his to render in his appointed time (the witnesses noting the significance — the sole right of God to avenge and repay, the judgment his prerogative, not man's, vengeance belongs to God, who will surely repay the wicked in his due time, their foot sliding when the day comes, the lesson — that men are not to take vengeance into their own hands, but to leave it to God who judges righteously, the certainty that God will repay, the day of calamity at hand, the slow but sure judgment, the truth that this is the ground of the Christian's forbearance, 'avenge not yourselves... for it is written, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord,' Romans 12:19, 'Vengeance belongeth unto me, I will recompense, saith the Lord,' Hebrews 10:30, the figure — the righteous judgment of God reserved to himself, the wicked's foot sliding in due time, 'thou castedst them down into destruction... in a moment,' Psalm 73:18-19, the truth that God's judgment, though it tarry, is certain and at hand, the day hastening, the figure of the divine vengeance that men must leave to God, committing their cause to him, the call to patience under wrong, knowing that God will repay, the warning that the wicked, secure now, shall surely fall, the figure of the prerogative of God to judge and repay, vengeance his alone); vengeance and recompence are God's alone, his right to render, taken up by Paul and Hebrews against private revenge (the divine prerogative, Romans 12:19; Hebrews 10:30) — the prerogative (vengeance is God's); 'their foot shall slide in due time: for the day of their calamity is at hand, and the things that shall come upon them make haste' (the wicked's foot will slide in God's due time, the judgment slow but sure and hastening, the certain recompence, Psalm 73:18-19) — the figure (the righteous judgment reserved to God, the providence, the vengeance, God's judging and repenting for his servants to follow, vv.36) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'to me belongeth vengeance' establish?

sides.position: The prerogative of God to judge and repay, vengeance his alone - men not to take vengeance into their own hands but leave it to God who judges righteously, the ground of the Christian's forbearance (Romans 12:19; Hebrews 10:30), the certain and hastening judgment that casts down the wicked in due time (Psalm 73:18-19)

themes: To me belongeth vengeance, and recompence; their foot shall slide in due time: for the day of their calamity is at hand, and the things that shall come upon them make haste
God's declaration that vengeance and recompence belong to him (the crux of the prerogative of God to judge and repay)
the sole right of God to avenge and repay, the judgment his and not man's, the ground of the Christian's forbearance and patience under wrong
the certain and hastening judgment that casts down the wicked in due time (Romans 12:19; Hebrews 10:30; Psalm 73:18-19)

schools.summary: That God would judge his people and have compassion when their strength was gone: 'for the LORD shall judge his people, and repent himself for his servants, when he seeth that their power is gone, and there is none shut up, or left' — God would judge but also relent toward his people when their strength was utterly gone (the for the LORD shall judge his people, God judging his people, the and repent himself for his servants, his compassion turning, the when he seeth that their power is gone, and there is none shut up, or left, when they are helpless, the figure, God's judgment turning to compassion when his people are brought low and helpless, the truth that God chastens but does not utterly forsake, his mercy moved when their strength fails, the figure of the compassion of God that pities his people in their extremity, 'he remembered that they were but flesh,' Psalm 78:39, the mercy that relents when the people are helpless, the judgment tempered, the turning of God toward his afflicted servants, vv.35); God judging yet relenting toward his servants (the mercy, Psalm 78:39) — the compassion (repenting for his servants); 'when he seeth that their power is gone, and there is none shut up, or left' (God's mercy moved when their strength is utterly gone, the compassion in extremity, the figure, the compassion that pities the helpless, the providence, the compassion, the mockery of their idols to follow, vv.37-38) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For the LORD shall judge his people, and repent himself for his servants, when he seeth that their power is gone, and there is none shut up, or left
that God would judge his people and have compassion when their strength was gone
God judging but also relenting toward his people when their strength was utterly gone
his compassion moved when they were helpless (Psalm 78:39)

schools.summary: The mockery of the idols in whom they trusted: 'and he shall say, Where are their gods, their rock in whom they trusted' — God taunts the idols, asking where the gods are that could not save (the and he shall say, Where are their gods, the taunt, the their rock in whom they trusted, the false rock, the figure, the mockery of the helpless idols, the false rock that could not save, the truth that the idols in whom they trusted are shown impotent in the day of need, the figure of the exposure of false gods, 'where are now thy gods?' Jeremiah 2:28, the taunt that unmasks the vanity of idols, the false rock that fails its worshippers, vv.31); God taunting the helpless idols (the mockery, Jeremiah 2:28) — the taunt (where are their gods); 'their rock in whom they trusted' (the false rock that could not save, the vanity of idols, the figure, the exposure of false gods, the providence, the taunt, the idols that ate the sacrifices unable to help to follow, vv.38) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he shall say, Where are their gods, their rock in whom they trusted
the mockery of the idols in whom they trusted
God taunting the helpless idols, asking where the gods are that could not save
the false rock that fails its worshippers (Jeremiah 2:28)

schools.summary: The idols challenged to rise and help: 'which did eat the fat of their sacrifices, and drank the wine of their drink offerings? let them rise up and help you, and be your protection' — the idols that received the offerings are challenged to rise and help, exposing their impotence (the which did eat the fat of their sacrifices, and drank the wine of their drink offerings?, the idols that received the offerings, the let them rise up and help you, and be your protection, the challenge to help, the figure, the idols challenged to do what they cannot, the mockery of their impotence, the truth that the gods who took the offerings can give no help, the vanity exposed, the figure of the helplessness of idols, called to save and unable, 'let them deliver thee in the time of thy trouble,' Jeremiah 2:28, the challenge that unmasks the dead gods, the idols that take but cannot give, vv.37); the idols that received the offerings (the false gods, Jeremiah 2:28) — the idols (that took the offerings); 'let them rise up and help you, and be your protection' (the idols challenged to help, unable, the impotence exposed, the figure, the helplessness of idols, the providence, the challenge, God's declaration that he alone is God to follow, vv.39) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Which did eat the fat of their sacrifices, and drank the wine of their drink offerings? let them rise up and help you, and be your protection
the idols challenged to rise and help
the idols that received the offerings challenged to do what they cannot
the mockery of the dead gods that take but cannot give (Jeremiah 2:28)

schools.summary: God's great declaration that he alone is God, who kills and makes alive: 'see now that I, even I, am he, and there is no god with me: I kill, and I make alive; I wound, and I heal: neither is there any that can deliver out of my hand' — the sole sovereignty — 'see now that I, even I, am he, and there is no god with me: I kill, and I make alive; I wound, and I heal: neither is there any that can deliver out of my hand': God declares himself the one and only God, sovereign over death and life, wounding and healing, from whose hand none can deliver (the witnesses noting the significance — the absolute and sole deity of the LORD, who alone holds the power of life and death, there is no god beside him, he kills and makes alive, wounds and heals, the giver and taker of life, the sovereign over all, the lesson — that God is supreme over all things, life and death in his hand, none able to resist or deliver from him, the great 'I, even I, am he,' the assertion of the sole deity against all idols, the power over life and death his alone, the truth that the LORD alone is God, who brings down to the grave and raises up, 'the LORD killeth, and maketh alive: he bringeth down to the grave, and bringeth up,' 1 Samuel 2:6, the figure — the sovereignty of God over life and death, fulfilled in Christ who has the keys of death, 'I am he that liveth, and was dead... and have the keys of hell and of death,' Revelation 1:18, the truth that none can deliver out of God's hand, his power irresistible, 'I will work, and who shall let it?' Isaiah 43:13, the figure of the absolute sovereignty of the one true God, the source of life and the appointer of death, the call to know and own that he alone is God, the warning that there is no escape from his hand, no rival to his power, the figure of the sole and sovereign deity of the LORD over life, death, and all things); the great assertion of the one and only God, no god beside him, against all idols (the sole deity, 1 Samuel 2:6) — the declaration (I am he, no god with me); 'I kill, and I make alive; I wound, and I heal: neither is there any that can deliver out of my hand' (God's sovereign power over death and life, wounding and healing, none able to deliver from his hand, fulfilled in Christ who has the keys of death, the irresistible sovereignty, Revelation 1:18; Isaiah 43:13) — the figure (the sovereignty over life and death, fulfilled in Christ, the providence, the declaration, God lifting his hand to swear to follow, vv.40) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'I, even I, am he... I kill, and I make alive' establish?

sides.position: The sole and sovereign deity of the LORD over life, death, and all things - no god beside him, sovereign over life and death, wounding and healing, the giver and taker of life (1 Samuel 2:6), a figure of Christ who has the keys of death (Revelation 1:18), his power irresistible and none able to deliver out of his hand (Isaiah 43:13)

themes: See now that I, even I, am he, and there is no god with me: I kill, and I make alive; I wound, and I heal: neither is there any that can deliver out of my hand

the absolute and sole deity of the LORD, no god beside him, sovereign over life and death, wounding and healing, the giver and taker of life
a figure of Christ who has the keys of death, his power irresistible and none able to deliver out of his hand (1 Samuel 2:6; Revelation 1:18; Isaiah 43:13)

schools.summary: God lifting his hand to swear that he lives for ever: 'for I lift up my hand to heaven, and say, I live for ever' — God swears by himself, lifting his hand, that he lives for ever (the for I lift up my hand to heaven, the gesture of the oath, the and say, I live for ever, the swearing by his eternal life, the figure, God swearing by his own eternal being, the most solemn oath, the truth that God, having none greater, swears by himself, his eternal life the surety, the figure of the divine oath, 'because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself,' Hebrews 6:13, the everlasting life of God the ground of the certainty of his word, the solemn oath that confirms the judgment, vv.41); God lifting his hand in the gesture of an oath (the swearing, Hebrews 6:13) — the gesture (lift up my hand); 'I live for ever' (God swearing by his eternal life, the surest oath, the figure, the divine oath by himself, the providence, the oath, the whetting of the sword of judgment to follow, vv.41-42) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God whetting his sword to render vengeance on his enemies: 'if I whet my glittering sword, and mine hand take hold on judgment; I will render vengeance to mine enemies, and will reward them that hate me' — God prepares his sword of judgment to avenge himself on his enemies (the if I whet my glittering sword, the sword sharpened, the and mine hand take hold on judgment, the hand grasping judgment, the I will render vengeance to mine enemies, and will reward them that hate me, the vengeance on his foes, the figure, God arming for judgment, the sword whetted against his enemies, the truth that God will surely render vengeance on those who hate him, the judgment prepared, the figure of the wrath of God against his enemies, 'his sword... bathed in heaven,' Isaiah 34:5, the certain recompence on the haters of God, the judgment that falls on his foes, vv.35); God whetting his sword for judgment (the wrath, Isaiah 34:5) — the sword (whetted); 'I will render vengeance to mine enemies, and will reward them that hate me' (the vengeance on his foes, the recompence on the haters of God, the figure, the wrath against his enemies, the providence, the vengeance, the sword drunk with blood to follow, vv.42) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God's arrows drunk with blood in judgment on the enemy: 'I will make mine arrows drunk with blood, and my sword shall devour flesh; and that with the blood of the slain and of the captives, from the beginning of revenges upon the enemy' — God's arrows and sword would be sated with the blood of his enemies (the I will make mine arrows drunk with blood, the arrows drunk with blood, the and my sword shall devour flesh, the sword devouring, the and that with the blood of the slain and of the captives, from the beginning of revenges upon the enemy, the blood of the foe, the figure, the terrible judgment of God on his enemies, the arrows and sword sated with blood, the truth that God's vengeance on his foes would be complete and dreadful, the figure of the day of God's wrath, 'the sword of the LORD is filled with blood,' Isaiah 34:6, the judgment that devours the enemy, the dreadful recompence on the foes of God, vv.41); the arrows and sword sated with the blood of the foe (the wrath, Isaiah 34:6) — the judgment (arrows drunk with blood); 'with the blood of the slain and of the captives, from the beginning of revenges upon the enemy' (the blood of the enemy in the day of vengeance, the dreadful judgment, the figure, the day of God's wrath, the providence, the judgment, the call to the nations to rejoice to follow, vv.43) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The call to the nations to rejoice with God's people: 'rejoice, O ye nations, with his people: for he will avenge the blood of his servants, and will render vengeance to his adversaries, and will be merciful unto his land, and to his people' — the calling of the Gentiles — 'rejoice, O ye nations, with his people: for he will avenge the blood of his servants, and will render vengeance to his adversaries, and will be merciful unto his land, and to his people': the nations are called to rejoice together with God's people, for God avenges his servants and shows mercy to his land (the witnesses noting the significance — the gathering of the Gentiles into the joy and salvation of God's people, the song closing not in judgment alone but in the rejoicing of the nations with Israel, the call to the nations to share in the joy, the avenging of the servants and the mercy on the land, the lesson — that the salvation of God reaches beyond Israel to the nations, who are bidden to rejoice with his people, the closing note of grace, the Gentiles included in the joy, the truth that this is a prophecy of the calling of the Gentiles, quoted by Paul as such, 'Rejoice, ye Gentiles, with his people,' Romans 15:10, the figure — the ingathering of the nations into the church, the song of Moses ending in the joy of Jew and Gentile together, 'that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy,' Romans 15:9, the truth that the mercy of God to his land and people overflows to the nations, the joy shared, the figure of the universal praise of the redeemed, the nations and Israel rejoicing as one, the avenging of the martyrs' blood and the mercy on the land the ground of the joy, the warning to the adversaries set against the joy of the gathered nations, the figure of the gathering of the Gentiles to share in the joy and salvation of God's people); the nations called to rejoice together with God's people, a prophecy of the calling of the Gentiles (the ingathering, Romans 15:10) — the call (rejoice, ye nations); 'for he will avenge the blood of his servants, and will render vengeance to his adversaries, and will be merciful unto his land, and to his people' (the avenging of the servants' blood and the mercy on the land the ground of the joy, the mercy overflowing to the nations, the Gentiles glorifying God for his mercy, Romans 15:9) — the figure (the ingathering of the nations into the church, the providence, the call, the close of the song and the charge to the people to follow, vv.44-47) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'rejoice, O ye nations, with his people' signify?

sides.position: The gathering of the Gentiles to share in the joy and salvation of Israel - the song closing in the rejoicing of the nations with God's people, a prophecy of the calling of the Gentiles quoted by Paul (Romans 15:10), the mercy of God overflowing to the nations that they might glorify him for his mercy (Romans 15:9)

schools.summary: Moses and Joshua speaking the song in the people's ears: 'and Moses came and spake all the words of this song in the ears of the people, he, and Hoshea the son of Nun' — Moses and Joshua recited the whole song before the people (the and Moses came and spake all the words of this song in the ears of the people, the song recited, the he, and Hoshea the son of Nun, with Joshua, the figure, the song spoken to all the people, Moses and his successor together, the truth that the song was delivered before all, the witness proclaimed, the figure of the joint witness of Moses and Joshua, the leader and his successor, the song recited entire, Joshua (Hoshea) by his side, the public delivery of the song, vv.30); Moses reciting the song to the people (the proclamation) — the recital (in their ears); 'he, and Hoshea the son of Nun' (Joshua joined in the recital, the joint witness, the figure, the leader and his successor together, the providence, the recital, the charge to set their hearts on the words to follow, vv.45-46) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Moses ending his speaking of all the words to Israel: 'and Moses made an end of speaking all these words to all Israel' — Moses finished delivering the song and all his words to the people (the and Moses made an end of speaking all these words to all Israel, the close of his speaking, the figure, the close of Moses's words, the song and the charge ended, the truth that Moses, having spoken all, made an end, his ministry of the word nearly closed, the figure of the faithful servant who delivers the whole counsel and then ends, 'I have not shunned to declare unto you all the counsel of God,' Acts 20:27, the conclusion of the song, the charge to follow, the end of Moses's speaking, vv.44); Moses finishing his speaking (the conclusion, Acts 20:27) — the close (made an end); 'all these words to all Israel' (all the words delivered to the people, the whole counsel, the figure, the faithful servant who declares all, the providence, the close, the charge to set their hearts on the words to follow, vv.46) — Gill, Ellicott.

schools.summary: The charge to set their hearts on the words and teach them to their children: 'and he said unto them, Set your hearts unto all the words which I testify among you this day, which ye shall command your children to observe to do, all the words of this law' — Moses charges them to lay the words to heart and teach their children to keep them (the and he said unto them, Set your hearts unto all the words which I testify among you this day, laying the words to heart, the which ye shall command your children to observe to do, all the words of this law, teaching the children, the figure, the charge to lay the word to heart and hand it down, the word to be loved and obeyed, the truth that the word is not to be heard only but laid to heart and taught to the children, the figure of the word taken to heart and handed down, 'these words... shall be in thine heart: and thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children,' Deuteronomy 6:6-7, the heart set on the word, the children taught, the transmission of the word to the next generation, vv.45); the charge to set the heart on the words (the heart, Deuteronomy 6:6) — the charge (set your hearts); 'ye shall command your children to observe to do' (the children taught to keep the word, the transmission, the figure, the word handed down, the providence, the charge, the word as their very life to follow, vv.47) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That the word of God is not a vain thing but their very life: 'for it is not a vain thing for you; because it is your life: and through this thing ye shall prolong your days in the land, whither ye go over Jordan to possess it' — the word as life — 'for it is not a vain thing for you; because it is your life: and through this thing ye shall prolong your days in the land': the law is no empty word but the very life of the people, the means of their long abiding in the land (the witnesses noting the significance — the word of God as the source of life and well-being, not a vain or empty thing but the people's very life, the law not to be slighted as trivial, but prized as life itself, the keeping of it the way of long life in the land, the lesson — that the word of God is the means of life, to be valued above all, not despised as vain, the obedience to the word the way of life and length of days, the word the spring of well-being, the truth that man lives by the word of God, 'man doth not live by bread only, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of the LORD,' Deuteronomy 8:3; Matthew 4:4, the figure — the word of God as the source of spiritual and eternal life, 'the words that I speak unto you, they are spirit, and they are life,' John 6:63, 'thou hast the words of eternal life,' John 6:68, the truth that the Scripture is no idle tale but the very life of the soul, to be received and obeyed, the figure of the life-giving word, by which the people live and prolong their days, the call to prize the word as life, not to count it vain, the warning that to slight the word is to forfeit life, the figure of the word of God as the very life of his people); the law is no empty word but the very life of the people, not to be slighted as trivial (the word the source of life, Deuteronomy 8:3) — the declaration (not vain, but your life); 'and through this thing ye shall prolong your days in the land, whither ye go over Jordan to possess it' (the keeping of the word the way of long abiding in the land, the obedience the spring of well-being, the word life to the soul, the life-giving word, John 6:63, 68) — the figure (the word as the source of spiritual and eternal life, the providence, the word, the command to go up Nebo to follow, vv.48-49) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'it is not a vain thing... because it is your life' establish?

sides.position: The word of God as the very life of his people - not a vain or empty thing but the source of life and well-being, the keeping of it the way of long life (Deuteronomy 8:3; Matthew 4:4), a figure of the word as the source of spiritual and eternal life, the words of Christ that are spirit and life (John 6:63, 68), the Scripture no idle tale but the life of the soul

themes: For it is not a vain thing for you; because it is your life: and through this thing ye shall prolong your days in the land, whither ye go over Jordan to possess it

a figure of the life-giving word of Christ that is spirit and life (Deuteronomy 8:3; Matthew 4:4; John 6:63)

schools.summary: God speaking to Moses that same day concerning his death: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses that selfsame day, saying' — on the very day he finished the song, God spoke to Moses of his approaching death (the and the LORD spake unto Moses that selfsame day, saying, God's word that day, the figure, God speaking to Moses on the very day, the command of his death following the song, the truth that immediately after the song, God called Moses to his end, the figure of the servant called to his rest the moment his work is done, the same day the song was given, the command to die, the word that opens the close of Moses's life, vv.49); God speaking to Moses (the command) — the word (God spake); 'that selfsame day, saying' (on the very day of the song, the command of his death following, the figure, the servant called to rest his work done, the providence, the word, the command to go up Nebo to follow, vv.49) — Gill.

schools.summary: The command to go up Nebo and behold the land: 'get thee up into this mountain Abarim, unto mount Nebo, which is in the land of Moab, that is over against Jericho; and behold the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel for a possession' — Moses was to climb Nebo and view the land he might not enter (the get thee up into this mountain Abarim, unto mount Nebo, the ascent of Nebo, the which is in the land of Moab, that is over against Jericho, the place, the and behold the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel for a possession, the land to be viewed, the figure, Moses to behold the land from afar but not enter it, the servant who sees the promise but enters not, the truth that Moses was granted the sight of the land though barred from it, the figure of the believer who sees the promise afar off, 'having seen them afar off,' Hebrews 11:13, the land viewed but not possessed by Moses, the law not bringing in, the command to ascend and behold, vv.50); the command to climb Nebo (the place) — the ascent (mount Nebo); 'behold the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel' (the land to be viewed but not entered, the promise seen afar, Hebrews 11:13, the figure, the believer who sees the promise afar off, the providence, the command, Moses to die in the mount to follow, vv.50) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Get thee up into this mountain Abarim, unto mount Nebo, which is in the land of Moab, that is over against Jericho; and behold the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel for a possession

schools.summary: The command that Moses die in the mount and be gathered to his people: 'and die in the mount whither thou goest up, and be gathered unto thy people; as Aaron thy brother died in mount Hor, and was gathered unto his people' — Moses was to die on Nebo and be gathered to his people, as Aaron died on Hor (the and die in the mount whither thou goest up, the death on the mount, the and be gathered unto thy people, gathered to his people, the as Aaron thy brother died in mount Hor, and was gathered unto his people, as Aaron, the figure, the death of Moses on the mount, gathered to his people as Aaron was, the truth that Moses, like Aaron, would die outside the land, gathered to his fathers, the figure of the saint gathered to his people, death a gathering not an ending, 'gathered unto his people,' the death on the mount, the soul gathered home, the parallel with Aaron's death, vv.49); Moses to die on the mount and be gathered to his people (the gathering) — the death (in the mount); 'as Aaron thy brother died in mount Hor, and was gathered unto his people' (the death like Aaron's, gathered to his people, the saint gathered home, the figure, death a gathering, not an ending, the providence, the death, the reason of the bar to follow, vv.51-52) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The reason Moses was barred from the land, the trespass at Meribah: 'because ye trespassed against me among the children of Israel at the waters of Meribah-Kadesh, in the wilderness of Zin; because ye sanctified me not in the midst of the children of Israel' — the failure to sanctify God — 'because ye trespassed against me among the children of Israel at the waters of Meribah-Kadesh, in the wilderness of Zin; because ye sanctified me not in the midst of the children of Israel': Moses (and Aaron) were barred from the land because at Meribah they failed to hallow God before the people (the witnesses noting the significance — the holiness of God that must be honoured even by his greatest servants, the sin of not sanctifying God in the eyes of the people, Moses's rash word and act at the rock dishonoured God's holiness before Israel, and even he must bear the consequence, the lesson — that God will be sanctified in all who come near him, his holiness not to be slighted, even by the meekest and most faithful, the severity that spared not Moses, that all might fear, the holiness of God upheld even at the cost of his servant's entrance, the truth that God is jealous for his holiness among his people, 'I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me,' Leviticus 10:3; Numbers 20:12, the figure — the strictness of God's holiness, that even one failure to hallow him before the people was visited, the truth that the greater the servant, the more is required, the more conspicuous the dishonour, the figure of the holiness of God that brooks no slighting, the warning to all who minister before him, the call to sanctify God in the eyes of the people, to give him the glory of his holiness, the sober reminder that God's grace to Moses did not exempt him from the consequence of dishonouring God's holiness, the figure of the holiness of God that even his greatest servant must honour); Moses's trespass at Meribah, the rash word and act at the rock, the occasion of the bar (the sin at the waters, Numbers 20:12) — the trespass (at Meribah); 'because ye sanctified me not in the midst of the children of Israel' (the failure to sanctify God before the people, the holiness of God not honoured, the cause of the exclusion, God sanctified in all who come near him, the holiness upheld, Leviticus 10:3) — the figure (the strictness of God's holiness, brooking no slighting, the providence, the reason, Moses to see the land but not enter to follow, vv.52) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why was Moses barred from the land?

sides.position: Because at Meribah he failed to sanctify God before the people - the holiness of God that must be honoured even by his greatest servants, Moses's failure to hallow God at the rock visited even on him, God jealous to be sanctified in all who come near him (Leviticus 10:3; Numbers 20:12), the strictness that spared not even the meekest servant that all might fear

themes: Because ye trespassed against me among the children of Israel at the waters of Meribah-Kadesh, in the wilderness of Zin; because ye sanctified me not in the midst of the children of Israel

schools.summary: That Moses should see the land but not enter it: 'yet thou shalt see the land before thee; but thou shalt not go thither unto the land which I give the children of Israel' — Moses was granted to see the land but not to enter it (the yet thou shalt see the land before thee, the sight granted, the but thou shalt not go thither unto the land which I give the children of Israel, the entrance barred, the figure, Moses granted the sight but denied the entrance, the servant who sees but enters not, the truth that Moses, the law-giver, brought the people to the border but might not enter, the law not bringing in, the figure of the believer who sees the promise but is gathered before its earthly fulfilment, 'these all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off,' Hebrews 11:13, the sight without the entrance, the meekness that submits, the close of Moses's life, the land seen, the rest in Christ, the law that brings to the border but Joshua that brings in); Moses granted the sight of the land (the view, Hebrews 11:13) — the sight (granted); 'thou shalt not go thither unto the land which I give the children of Israel' (the entrance denied, the servant who sees but enters not, the law not bringing in, the figure, the believer who sees the promise afar off, the providence, the bar, the blessing of the tribes to follow, ch.33) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.
